text:
Multitask prompted finetuning (MTF) has been shown to drastically improve the zero-shot generalization performance of large language models. The majority of the literature has so far focused exclusively on English language models and datasets. Here, we study MTF for multilingual language models and train parts of the BLOOM and mT5 model families to create two new sets of models called BLOOMZ and mT0.

We examine the effectiveness of different approaches to multi-language adaptation. Our experiments show that adapting large multilingual language models to English by finetuning on English tasks with English prompts not only helps them learn information about English but also enables them to utilize knowledge that they learned about non-English language populations during pretraining. In addition, adapting the models to multi-language tasks with English prompts significantly outperforms baselines across both English and non-English evaluation sets, achieving state-of-the-art performance on a number of zero-shot benchmarks. We also investigate the effects of using machine-translated prompts to match the language of the prompt to the language of the dataset and find that using translated prompts can aid in learn- ing how to utilize human-written prompts in specific languages.

Our models surprisingly apply to tasks in languages in which they were not intentionally trained on. This implies that the models learn language-agnostic, high level task capabilities. To help advance the field, we release xP3: a huge collection of fully-supervised datasets of varying sizes for 46 languages with English and machine-translated prompts. Code, datasets and models released for use.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written

Code, datasets and models released for use.
